1:
On Matthew 7:1 — “Judge not, that ye be not judged” — the witnesses agree this is no absolute ban on all judgment but a cure for rash, censorious, uncharitable judging, and they labour to reconcile it with the rebuke that Scripture elsewhere commands.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the prohibition narrowly. Barnes: it “refers to rash, censorious, and unjust judgment” (Romans 2:1; Luke 6:37), not to the magistrate’s lawful office nor to forming a necessary opinion of evil conduct, but to “a habit of forming a judgment hastily, harshly, and without allowance for every palliating circumstance.” Robertson notes our word “critic” comes from this verb (to separate, discriminate): discrimination is needful, but “prejudice… captious criticism” is forbidden. Wesley sums the limits — judge no man “without full, clear, certain knowledge, without absolute necessity, without tender love.” Gill carefully excepts judgment in the civil courts, in the churches, and the private opinion that does a man no harm, condemning only “rash judgment… interpreting men’s words and deeds to the worst sense.”

The Latin and Greek fathers press the same balance. Augustine reads it as a command to “put the best interpretation” on doubtful actions, since of plainly evil deeds — “adulteries, blasphemies, and the like” — we may rightly judge; it is “indifferent actions” that we must not condemn. Chrysostom: be “not a bitter judge… correct him indeed, but… as a physician applying a remedy,” the prohibition lying chiefly on those “full of great evils” who judge others “for very small evils.” John Chrysostom harmonizes it with “reprove, rebuke, exhort” (2 Timothy 4:2) and Paul’s “who art thou that judgest another man’s servant?” (Romans 14:4; 10), and Jerome instances Paul judging the Corinthian and Peter convicting Ananias.

The Reformed Calvin reads it as the cure of “a disease which appears to be natural to us all” — the strange enjoyment of “inquiring into other people’s faults” while overlooking our own. The Puritan Henry hears a reproof of the Pharisees, “rigid and severe… in condemning all about them.” One outlier is Hilary, who takes “judge not” of judging God touching his promises, where “we have begun to sin if we have pronounced any judgment at all.”
2:
On Matthew 7:2 — “with what judgment ye judge, ye shall be judged: and with what measure ye mete, it shall be measured to you again” — the witnesses agree Christ adopts a familiar Jewish proverb to teach a law of retribution.

The grammatical-historical commentators document the saying. Lightfoot calls it “a very common proverb among the Jews” — “in the measure that a man measureth, others measure to him” — illustrated in the tractate Sotah “by various examples.” Barnes notes Christ “did not hesitate to adopt it as conveying his own sentiments,” for it bears both on “the way in which men will judge of us” and on the rule by which God will judge (2 Samuel 22:27; Mark 4:24; James 2:13). Clarke draws the natural consequence: “he who is severe on others will naturally excite their severity against himself,” the censures we suffer being often “the just reward of those which we have dealt out.”

Ellicott guards the principle from misunderstanding: “the unjust judgment of man does not bring upon us a divine judgment which is also unjust; but the severity which we have unjustly meted out to others becomes, by a retributive law, the measure of that which is justly dealt out to us.” The Wesleyan Wesley reads the verse with awe: “so we may, as it were, choose for ourselves, whether God shall be severe or merciful to us” — for “they must expect judgment without mercy who have showed no mercy.” The shared point is sobering: our own standard of censure becomes the standard of our own reckoning, before both God and men. Gill adduces the kindred Jewish maxims, and Barnes notes the rule applies “no less to the way in which men will judge of us than to the rule by which God will judge us” (Judges 1:7). Thus the proverb is no mere worldly prudence but a window into the divine economy of judgment, where the harsh and unmerciful prepare a hard measure for themselves, and the candid and benevolent are met, by God and man alike, with the gentleness they showed.
3:
On Matthew 7:3 — “why beholdest thou the mote that is in thy brother’s eye, but considerest not the beam that is in thine own eye?” — the witnesses agree on the proverb’s point (men are keen-sighted to others’ small faults, blind to their own great ones) and add moral and allegorical depth.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the image and its currency. Robertson glosses the “mote” (karphos) as “a piece of dried wood or chaff, splinter… a very small particle that may irritate,” and the “beam” (dokos) as a “joist, rafter, plank” — “probably a current proverb,” quoting the Arabic “how seest thou the splinter in thy brother’s eye, and seest not the cross-beam in thine own?” Clarke prefers “splinter” to “mote,” as bearing “some analogy to beam.” Ellicott finds the same proverb in Rabbi Tarphon and in the satires of every country — “the Gracchi complain of sedition, and Clodius accuses others of adultery” — recalling Burns’s wish “to see oursels as others see us.” Barnes: “we are much more quick and acute to judge of small offences in others than of much larger offences in ourselves.”

The Latin and Greek fathers read it morally. Augustine measures the difference of beam and mote by that between “anger and hatred,” hatred being “anger become inveterate,” so that one may rebuke a brother’s lesser fault with a greater sin of settled hate. Chrysostom marks how men flay a monk for “a superfluous garment” while themselves given to excess. Pseudo-Chrysostom applies it to teachers, whose sin is the greater “beam,” and Hilary reads the beam as the heavy sin against the Spirit, the unbeliever’s distrust of God’s promises.

The Reformed Calvin names the doubled fault of hypocrites — “too quick-sighted in discerning the faults of others” yet “ingenious in finding apologies” for their own — both “the excessive sagacity which arises from a defect of charity” and “the indulgence by which we… cherish our own sins.” Gill defines the “mote” as “little sins, comparatively speaking… youthful follies, human frailties… light faults in comparison of others,” which, though not to be vindicated, are not to be “severely looked upon and chastised” by one guilty of greater. Wesley puts the question pointedly: “why do you open your eyes to any fault of your brother, while you yourself are guilty of a much greater?” — the beam and mote being “proverbially used by the Jews to denote the one small infirmities, the other gross, palpable faults.”
4:
On Matthew 7:4 — “how wilt thou say to thy brother, Let me pull out the mote out of thine eye; and, behold, a beam is in thine own eye?” — the comment is brief and unanimous: the question expresses the effrontery of the censorious man who would correct a small fault while harbouring a greater.

The Reformed Gill reads it “not so much an interrogation as an expression of admiration” (astonishment) “at the front and impudence of such censorious remarkers and rigid observators,” who, not content to point at others’ faults, “take upon them to reprove them in a very magisterial way.” Ellicott renders the force exactly — “how wilt thou have the face to say” — capturing the boldness Christ exposes.

The grammatical-historical Lightfoot shows the saying “was a known proverb among them,” citing the rabbinic exchange: when one judge said “cast out the mote out of thine eye,” the other answered, “cast out the beam out of thine own”; and Rabbi Tarphon’s lament, “I wonder whether there be any in this age that will receive reproof.” The point, across the voices, is that the would-be reprover’s own greater guilt strips him of all standing: his offer to help is not charity but presumption, and his brother may justly turn the rebuke back upon him. The verse thus prepares for the remedy of the next, that self-judgment must precede the judging of others. Lightfoot’s rabbinic citation sharpens the point: in “the generation which judged their judges,” the reproof “cast out the mote” was met with “cast out the beam out of thine own eye,” so that mutual fault-finding stalled all correction. Augustine had warned that one may “correct him with settled hate” while the offender merely “sinned in anger” — the reprover’s beam of hatred far outweighing the brother’s mote. The censorious man, in short, disqualifies himself not by noticing the fault but by his own greater, unrepented guilt. Ellicott captures the tone exactly — “how wilt thou have the face to say” — for the offer to extract a brother’s mote, made by one with a beam in his own eye, is not help but effrontery, and answers the question of v.3 with the obvious absurdity of the attempt.
5:
On Matthew 7:5 — “Thou hypocrite, first cast out the beam out of thine own eye; and then shalt thou see clearly to cast out the mote out of thy brother’s eye” — the witnesses agree the verse both condemns censorious self-righteousness and prescribes the right order of reform: self first, then the brother.

On the name “hypocrite,” the Reformed Gill notes Christ “rightly” so calls the man “very free in remarking and reproving other men’s sins, and covering his own,” one end of whose censures is “that he might be thought to be holier than he is” — a stroke aimed plainly at the scribes and Pharisees. Ellicott deepens it: the hypocrisy “is all the greater because it does not know itself to be hypocritical,” the man acting the reformer while needing reform most. Clarke adds that the hypocrite, to keep up his assumed character, “must reprove sin” in others, his hidden corruption “more abominable in the sight of God” than open profligacy.

On the remedy, the grammatical-historical commentators stress the new clear sight. Robertson glosses “see clearly” (diablepseis, to “look through, penetrate”) against the mere gazing of v.3: “get the log out of your eye and you will see clearly how to help the brother.” Barnes: by “first amending our own faults… we can consistently advance to correct the faults of others,” with no hypocrisy in our conduct. Ellicott marks that here “the teaching of the Sermon on the Mount rises far above” mere maxims, giving “a new motive to… self-scrutiny”: only when we have “wrestled with and overcome our own besetting sins” are we fit to deal with another’s. Wesley: “it is mere hypocrisy to pretend zeal for the amendment of others while we have none for our own.” Chrysostom notes that Christ, intending “to denounce a great sin, begins with an epithet of reproach” — “thou hypocrite” — because each “knows better the things of himself than the things of others, and sees more the things that be great” in his neighbour than in himself. Christ, all agree, does not forbid helping the brother — the mote is still to be removed — but requires that the helper first be cleansed himself, that he may “see clearly” and act in charity rather than contempt. (The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note attached here strays to the Lord’s Prayer.)
6:
On Matthew 7:6 — “Give not that which is holy unto the dogs, neither cast ye your pearls before swine” — the witnesses agree the saying warns against profaning sacred things on those who will only despise them, and debate what is meant by the holy thing.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the “holy.” Ellicott and Robertson (with Trench) take it as the sacrificial flesh of Leviticus 22:6–7, of which no unclean person or beast might eat, so that to fling it to dogs “would have seemed… the greatest of all profanations” (compare Exodus 22:31); the “pearls,” the costliest of jewels (Matthew 13:46; 1 Timothy 2:9), stand for the truths of the gospel. Barnes reads “dogs” and “swine” as men “of peculiar sourness and malignity” who meet the truth “like growling and quarrelsome curs” (2 Peter 2:22). Clarke and Wesley note the “transposition” — the holy thing answering to the dogs that “turn and rend,” the pearls to the swine that “trample under foot.”

The fathers add the moral and sacramental sense. Augustine takes the holy thing as “all that it were impiety to corrupt” and the pearls as “spiritual things to be highly esteemed”; Pseudo-Chrysostom reads it of priests not communicating “the things of God” (even baptism) to enemies of the faith, though commanded to love them with temporal goods. Chrysostom warns that the unworthy “feign meekness that they may learn; and when they have learned, they attack.” Rabanus distinguishes dogs (returned to their vomit) from swine (still wallowing).

The Reformed Calvin raises the difficulty that Christ also bids preach to all (Mark 16:15): the rule is not against offering the gospel widely but reminds teachers “to reserve the treasure of heavenly wisdom for the children of God” and not cast it before “profane despisers” once they show themselves hardened. The Gloss softens it with “lest perchance,” allowing that some may yet “wisely turn from their uncleanness.” Wesley draws the pastoral application: “talk not of the deep things of God to those whom you know to be wallowing in sin… talk not of perfection to the former, nor of your experience to the latter” — yet our Lord “does in nowise forbid us to reprove, as occasion is, both the one and the other.” Chrysostom warns that the unworthy, given holy things, “turn” — “they feign meekness that they may learn; and when they have learned, they attack.” The discernment enjoined, then, is not contempt for the lost but a sober reckoning with hardened malice that would only trample and rend the holy thing offered.
7:
On Matthew 7:7 — “Ask, and it shall be given you; seek, and ye shall find; knock, and it shall be opened” — the witnesses agree this is an exhortation to earnest, persevering prayer, and several see a deliberate ascent in the three verbs.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw out the rising intensity. Clarke hears in the three words “the ideas of want, loss, and earnestness”: ask as a destitute beggar, seek as one who has lost his soul and leaves “no stone unturned,” knock as one in earnest with eternity at hand. Barnes notes “knock” is drawn from seeking admittance at a door (Luke 11:7; Revelation 3:20), all signifying “earnestness, diligence, and perseverance,” with the implied conditions that we ask with a right spirit and for fitting things (1 John 5:14). Ellicott supplies the latent sequence: if reforming self and others is so hard, where shall we find courage and wisdom? — “in prayer for those gifts.”

The fathers deepen the three. Augustine: asking gains “healthiness of soul” to fulfil the commands, seeking “pertains to the discovery of the truth,” and to the knocker alone is actual possession opened. The Gloss: “we ask with faith, we seek with hope, we knock with love.” Chrysostom adds that to seek is to cast all else from the mind, and to knock is to come “with vehemence and warm soul.” The Alexandrian Origen joins “seek and ye shall find” to the merchant seeking goodly pearls, who counts all loss “that I may win Christ,” the one precious pearl (Philippians 3:8).

The Reformed Calvin notes the threefold form is no “superfluity of language” but perseverance to “rouse us from our inactivity,” the promises added because “nothing is better adapted to excite us to prayer than a full conviction that we shall be heard” — for prayer “unaccompanied by faith is an idle and unmeaning ceremony.” The Puritan Henry: “pray, and pray again… make a business of prayer, and be earnest in it.” Wesley adds that we may even “ask” for our enemies, where “there can be no such danger” (Luke 11:9).
8:
On Matthew 7:8 — “for every one that asketh receiveth; and he that seeketh findeth; and to him that knocketh it shall be opened” — the witnesses agree the promise is universal and sure to all who pray aright, the ground that moves us to ask.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the certainty. Clarke: “prayer is always heard after one manner or other… no soul can pray in vain that prays as directed,” the truth and faithfulness of Christ “pledged for its success” — “ye shall receive, ye shall find, it shall be opened” being “as strongly binding on the side of God as ‘thou shalt do no murder’ is on the side of man.” Barnes anchors the promise in Scripture’s repeated assurances (Psalm 81:10; John 16:24; 1 John 3:22). Gill marks the impartiality: “God is no respecter of persons; whoever makes application… Jew or Gentile, rich or poor… provided he asks aright… shall not lose his labour.”

The Reformed Calvin reads the verse as Christ presenting “the grace of his Father to those who pray”: God is “of himself prepared to listen to us,” his riches “at our command, provided we ask them,” so that those who, “destitute of what is necessary, yet do not resort to this remedy for their poverty, are justly punished for their slothfulness.” The settled sense across the witnesses is that the universal “every one” leaves no praying soul excluded: the promise turns on the asking, and the only failure is the failure to ask, or to ask “aright, and what is agreeable to God’s will” (Wesley). Even when believers sleep, Calvin adds, God “keeps watch over their salvation, and anticipates their wishes” — how much more will he answer the earnest cry. Clarke reads the three verbs as a rising scale of assurance: “ye shall receive… ye shall find… it shall be opened,” words “as strongly binding on the side of God” as the commandments are on ours, so that “bring Christ’s word and Christ’s sacrifice with thee, and not one of Heaven’s blessings can be denied thee.” The universal “every one,” the witnesses agree, both excludes none who truly seek and condemns the slothful soul that, destitute of all, will not so much as ask.
9:
On Matthew 7:9 — “what man is there of you, whom if his son ask bread, will he give him a stone?” — the witnesses agree this is an argument from the less to the greater, from a human father’s love to God’s, and several read the bread and stone allegorically.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the homely force. Ellicott observes the illustration was “addressed to the peasants of Galilee, who found in fish and bread… the staple of their daily food.” Robertson adds that “some stones look like loaves of bread,” the very likeness the devil exploited (Matthew 4:3). Clarke and Lightfoot recall the proverb of “stony bread” (Seneca’s panem lapidosum), a benefit “grudgingly given by an avaricious man,” and Plautus’s figure of one who “in one hand brings a stone, and stretches out bread in the other.”

The fathers draw spiritual meanings. Augustine notes that men are called “evil” as “lovers of this age,” yet the good gifts they give are truly goods, “temporal goods… such as pertain to this weak life,” while “that good thing which makes men good is God.” Pseudo-Chrysostom reads the loaf as “the word concerning the knowledge of God” and the stone as “all falsehood that has a stumbling-block”; Remigius and Rabanus expand the figures — bread as charity, fish as faith born of baptism, against the stone of hatred and the serpent of treachery.

The Reformed Calvin states the logic: “self-love renders us malicious… but this vice yields to the stronger feelings of a father’s love,” a love that is itself “a portion of God’s goodness” (Ephesians 3:15) — “if the little drops produce such beneficence, what ought we to expect from the inexhaustible ocean?” Gill marks that the man supposed is “a father… unless he is become a mere brute, and devoid of all humanity.” The verse thus turns the instinct of earthly fatherhood into a parable of the heavenly Father’s readiness to give. Pseudo-Chrysostom adds that the comparison meets despair: lest a man, “weighing his own sins, should despair of obtaining, and so never take in hand to ask,” Christ sets before him “the relation between father and son, that… we may hope because of God’s fatherly goodness.” Chrysostom marks the two things needful in prayer — “that he ask earnestly, and that he ask such things as he ought,” namely spiritual things, as Solomon, asking aright, “received speedily.”
10:
On Matthew 7:10 — “Or if he ask a fish, will he give him a serpent?” — the comment is brief and chiefly grammatical-historical, completing the homely pair of the previous verse.

Gill notes the serpent is named because it is “somewhat like a fish, especially an eel,” just as a stone may resemble a loaf, so that the picture is of a father who would mock his child’s hunger with a hurtful counterfeit of the very thing asked. He observes that “fish and bread are mentioned, because these were common food… particularly in Galilee, a fish country, where Christ now was, and from whence he had called his disciples, who were fishermen.” Gill adds the Lukan third member — “if he shall ask an egg, will he offer him a scorpion?” (Luke 11:12) — all used “to show the absurdity and inhumanity of such conduct,” which is “not to be found among men, unless it be among monsters in nature.”

Robertson confirms the pairing: “fish, common article of food, and water-snakes could easily be substituted,” noting the slight anacoluthon in the Greek sentence. The cumulative force of vv.9–10, on every reading, is that no natural father, however flawed, would answer a child’s trustful request with something useless or venomous; the contrast prepares the “how much more” of the next verse, where the argument rises from the grudging or limited goodness of earthly parents to the boundless generosity of God, who gives not stones and serpents but the very bread of life and the gift of his Spirit to those who ask him. Remigius reads the figures spiritually — the fish as “the word concerning Christ” or the water of baptism, the serpent as “the wiles of the Devil, or unbelief” — so that we need not fear, asking faith of our Father, that he will “suffer us to die of the poison of unbelief.” The very absurdity of a father’s cruelty measures, by contrast, the certainty of God’s kindness. Gill stresses that such conduct is “not to be found among men, unless it be among monsters in nature” — and if not among flawed men, how much less in the Father of mercies, who answers the trustful asking of his children with good and not with harm.
11:
On Matthew 7:11 — “if ye then, being evil, know how to give good gifts unto your children, how much more shall your Father… give good things to them that ask him?” — the witnesses agree the verse crowns the argument from the less to the greater, and discuss both the word “evil” and the “good things” promised.

On “evil,” the witnesses balance human depravity against natural affection. Ellicott: the words “at once recognise the fact of man’s depravity, and assert that it is not total”; in the midst of our evil remains “that element of natural and pure affection which makes the fatherhood of men a fit parable of the Fatherhood of God.” Chrysostom guards that Christ speaks “not detracting from human nature… but he calls paternal love ‘evil’ when compared with his own goodness,” such is “the superabundance of his love.” Clarke reads ponēroi ontes strongly — “radically depraved” — yet finds in the verse such a picture of God’s goodness that “God is Love.” Gill takes the chief evil in view to be the Jews’ covetousness.

On the “good things,” the grammatical-historical and Reformed voices stress the spiritual gift. Ellicott and Clarke note Luke’s parallel names the one gift of the Holy Spirit (Luke 11:13), which “in its sevenfold diversity includes them all.” Calvin presses that Christ names “good things” so “that believers may not give way to foolish and improper desires in prayer”: we must “submit our desires to the will of God, that he may give us nothing more than he knows to be advantageous,” for “all our affections being blind, the rule of prayer must be sought” from him. The Wesleyan Wesley adds the condition — God gives “to them that ask him, but on this condition, that ye follow the example of his goodness, by doing to all as ye would they should do to you” — so binding the promise to the golden rule that follows. Calvin presses that we must “submit our desires to the will of God” and not think he “takes no notice of us when he does not answer our wishes,” for “he has a right to distinguish what we actually need,” all our affections being “blind.” Robertson notes the “how much more” is an argument “Jesus is fond of” — the lesser certainty of a flawed father’s love guaranteeing the greater certainty of the Father’s. (A stray Cyril of Jerusalem fragment on the angels’ vision of God is attached here.)
12:
On Matthew 7:12 — “all things whatsoever ye would that men should do to you, do ye even so to them: for this is the law and the prophets” — the witnesses agree this “golden rule” sums the whole duty toward neighbour, and several note its positive form and its place in the discourse.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark its distinctiveness. Robertson notes the negative form was known to Hillel, Philo, Confucius, and stands in Tobit (Luke 6:31), but “Jesus puts it in positive form” — “the distilled essence of that fulfilment taught in the sermon.” Lightfoot and Ellicott tell the story of the Gentile who asked Hillel to teach the law “standing upon one foot,” and was answered, “that which is odious to thyself, do it not to thy neighbour: for this is the whole law.” Barnes calls it the rule that is to morals “what the balance-wheel is to machinery,” able to “destroy avarice, envy, treachery… and murder.” Clarke guards against abuse — the prisoner may not bid the judge release him on this rule, for both act not “as private men” but under public order.

The fathers and Reformed set it in context. Pseudo-Chrysostom and Chrysostom show the “therefore” gathers up the command not to judge: if you would be heard in prayer, “do this also.” Cyprian: the Lord “summed up all his commands in one precept.” Augustine reads it as owning our equal, since the beggar before God should own the beggar before himself. The Puritan Henry makes it the law of justice “fitly subjoined to the law of prayer.” The Wesleyan Wesley sums the doctrinal part of the sermon here: “the whole is comprised in one word, Imitate the God of love” — answering exactly to Matthew 5:17. Chrysostom notes the careful wording: not “whatsoever you would have done for you by God, do for your neighbour” (lest we plead inability), but “whatsoever you would have done to you by your fellow-servant” — a measure within every man’s reach. Gill and Ellicott alike call it a “golden rule” binding on all mankind, Jew and Gentile, the very “balance-wheel” of the moral world that would, if kept, “prevent all irregularity of movement.”
13:
On Matthew 7:13 — “Enter ye in at the strait gate… broad is the way that leadeth to destruction” — the witnesses agree the image is of two ways and two gates, the way of life being narrow and the way of ruin broad.

The grammatical-historical commentators correct a common confusion. Robertson and Barnes distinguish “strait” (narrow, pent-up) from “straight” (not crooked): it is “the narrow gate,” the way to life being “pent up, narrow, close, and not obviously entered,” while the way to death is “open, broad, and thronged.” Robertson notes the figure of the Two Ways had wide currency in Jewish and Christian writing (Deuteronomy 30:19; Jeremiah 21:8; the Didache and Barnabas), and Ellicott and Robertson both cite the Tablet of Cebes — “a certain small door, and a pathway before the door… the way that leadeth to true discipline.” Lightfoot adds the rabbinic measures of a public way (sixteen cubits) and a private one (four).

The fathers and Reformed read the difficulty and its solace. Chrysostom reconciles the “strait” way with the “easy yoke” (Matthew 11:30): the toil is borne for a good end, life, as sailors make light of storms for perishable gain. Jerome notes “the broad way needs no search… it is the way of all who go astray,” while many who find the narrow way “desert midway,” caught by the world’s pleasures. The Reformed Gill takes the strait gate as Christ himself, “the door” into the church and to the Father; Calvin reads it that the disciple “must prepare to walk along a thorny” path, the bitterness soothed “by a joyful remuneration.” Wesley: the strait gate is “the holiness described in the foregoing chapters,” the many going the broad way because “they like a wider gate,” needing not to seek it since “they come to it of course” (Luke 13:24). Augustine connects the strait gate to the single, purified heart Christ had been forming throughout the sermon, “such an eye as may discern the narrow way”; and the Gloss owns that though “love be wide, yet it leads men… through difficult and steep ways,” it being hard “to cast aside all other things, and to love One only.”
14:
On Matthew 7:14 — “because strait is the gate, and narrow is the way, which leadeth unto life, and few there be that find it” — the witnesses dwell on the narrowness of the way, the first naming of “life,” and the sobering “few.”

The grammatical-historical commentators render the image vividly. Ellicott glosses “narrow” as “pressed, or hemmed in between walls or rocks, like the pathway in a mountain gorge,” and marks this as “the first passage in our Lord’s recorded teaching in which the word ‘life’ appears as summing up all the blessedness of the kingdom” — a thought later developed into “eternal” life and the knowledge of God (John 17:3). Clarke prefers the reading “how strait is the gate” to “because,” as more forcibly pointing out the difficulty, marvelling that men are “unwilling to give up their worldly interests to secure their everlasting salvation.” Gill describes the way as “hedged up on each side with afflictions and tribulations,” like the strait place where Balaam’s angel stood, with “no turning to the right hand or the left.”

On the “few,” Ellicott observes “the sad contrast between the many and the few runs through all our Lord’s teaching”: he comes to “save the world,” yet those chosen out of it are “but as a little flock.” Lightfoot gathers the many scriptural and rabbinic uses of “gate” — the gate of repentance, of prayer, of tears — noting that since the Temple fell “the gates of prayer were shut, but the gates of tears were not shut.” The verse thus joins a hard summons with a glad promise: the way is narrow and the travellers few, but it alone “leadeth unto life.” Barnes draws the encouragement that, narrow though it be, the way is open and the gate may be entered by all who will; the fewness is not God’s grudging but men’s unwillingness to forsake the broad and thronged road. Gill’s “great encouragement to walk on in it” is precisely that “it is that way” which leads to life — the affliction that hedges it being light beside the end to which it brings the traveller.
15:
On Matthew 7:15 — “Beware of false prophets, which come to you in sheep’s clothing, but inwardly they are ravening wolves” — the witnesses agree the warning is against false teachers, and debate whether heretics or corrupt-living teachers are chiefly meant.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the danger. Barnes: a false prophet is “a teacher of incorrect doctrine, or one falsely… laying claim to Divine inspiration”; to come “in sheep’s clothing” is “to assume the appearance of sanctity and innocence when the heart is evil.” Robertson traces the type from the Old Testament prophets through Judaizers (2 Corinthians 11:13) and Gnostics (1 John 4:1; 1 Timothy 4:1), “greedy for power, gain, self.” Lightfoot reads “sheep’s clothing” as the very skins of sheep, so that they “might seem sheep” outwardly. Clarke brands those who “preach for hire… no other than ravenous wolves.”

The chief debate is the target. Chrysostom thinks Christ aims “not so much at the heretic as at those who, while their life is corrupt, yet wear an outward face of virtuousness,” since among heretics “it is possible to find a good life” but not among these. Jerome and Augustine apply it “specially… to heretics, who by… chastity and fasting surround themselves with a garment of sanctity” while “poisoned” within. Gregory adds that the hypocrite, restrained in peaceful times, “strips himself of his sheep’s skin” when trial comes. The Reformed Calvin warns the Church “would be exposed to various impositions,” Satan ever “laying snares to entrap ignorant and heedless persons,” and Gill suspects a glance at the Scribes and Pharisees with their doctrines “repugnant to the word of God.” Wesley: false prophets are all “who teach any other way than that our Lord hath here marked out,” describing “a broad way to heaven” — their prophesying, “rather than their walking in it themselves,” being chiefly meant. Pseudo-Chrysostom places the warning after the precepts on secret alms, prayer, and fasting, since “all these things may be done in hypocrisy.” The sheep’s clothing, the witnesses agree, is precisely the outward show of these religious duties, under which the wolf’s appetite for “power, gain, self” is hidden until trial or fruit lays it bare.
16:
On Matthew 7:16 — “Ye shall know them by their fruits. Do men gather grapes of thorns, or figs of thistles?” — the witnesses agree Christ supplies the test of the false prophet, and divide on whether the “fruits” are works or doctrines.

The grammatical-historical commentators take the test broadly. Clarke: “fruits… are taken for works of any kind” — “a man’s works are the tongue of his heart, and tell honestly whether he is inwardly corrupt or pure,” so that “the judgment formed of a man by his general conduct is a safe one.” Barnes: men judge a tree “not by its leaves, or bark, or flowers, but by the fruit”; so a profession may be fair, but “the conduct… is to determine the nature of the principles” (Matthew 12:33). Robertson notes the verb is the strengthened “fully know” (epignōsesthe).

Here a real division opens. Ellicott reports that those who stress “the ethical side of religion” see the fruits as “the practical outcome of doctrine in life, character, and deeds,” while others, “in constant dread of heresy, dwell on doctrines… lest good works be but the sheep’s clothing that hides the heretic wolf” — and he judges the analogy of Scripture, which calls good works “the fruits of faith,” favours the ethical reading. The Reformed Gill, by contrast, takes the “fruits” chiefly as “their doctrines, and the effects of them,” since a false teacher may keep “the form of godliness” in conduct. Calvin reads the mark as given to save us from holding “all teachers suspected” and shutting our ears to all alike. The Wesleyan Wesley offers a plain test for “the weakest capacity”: “true prophets convert sinners to God… false prophets do not,” their grand mark being “not turning men from the power of Satan to God” (Luke 6:44). Calvin reads the mark as a mercy that saves us from two errors — holding “all teachers suspected” and shutting our ears to all alike, or receiving every teacher without trial — so “that our reverence for the Gospel… may not be diminished.” Whether the fruit be weighed as life or as doctrine, the witnesses agree it furnishes a test plain and sure enough for the simplest believer to apply.
17:
On Matthew 7:17 — “every good tree bringeth forth good fruit; but a corrupt tree bringeth forth evil fruit” — the witnesses agree the tree is good before its fruit, so that the fruit reveals but does not make the tree; they divide on whether the figure speaks of doctrine or of personal character.

The Reformed Gill draws the order out carefully: the good tree “is first good, and then puts forth good fruit; it is not the fruit that makes the tree good, but makes it appear to be so.” So a good man does good works, but “is so before he performs” them, “or he would never be able to do them”; and so a good preacher, with “an experimental knowledge of the doctrines of the Gospel,” delivers sound doctrine. The grammatical-historical Barnes clarifies that “corrupt” does not mean a tree once good now vitiated, but “a tree of a useless character… that produces nothing beneficial” (Luke 6:43). Clarke reads the lesson against hypocrisy: as the thorn yields only thorns, “the profession of godliness, while the life is ungodly, is imposture” — “a man cannot be a saint and a sinner at the same time” — yet only God can “pluck up the vicious tree, create a good heart.”

The chief nuance is Wesley’s, who insists “the goodness or badness here mentioned respects the doctrine, rather than the personal character. For a bad man preaching the good doctrine here delivered is sometimes an instrument of converting sinners to God” — though he affirms that none are true prophets “who do not” so convert. Across the voices, the figure presses the same searching truth applied to teachers: what a man habitually produces — in doctrine and in life — betrays the root from which he grows, for a sound root cannot but bear, in time, its own kind of fruit. Gill presses the order once more: as a good man “is so before he performs good works… or he would never be able to do them,” so the fruit is the evidence, never the cause, of the tree’s nature. Clarke turns it to self-examination — since “every heart is naturally vicious,” only God “can pluck up the vicious tree, create a good heart, plant, cultivate” the good. (The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note attached here strays to the treasures and moths of Matthew 6:19.)
18:
On Matthew 7:18 — “A good tree cannot bring forth evil fruit, neither can a corrupt tree bring forth good fruit” — the witnesses press the impossibility stated, again dividing over doctrine and character.

The Reformed Gill applies it to the faithful teacher: a man grounded in grace and led by the Spirit “cannot knowingly deliver… any doctrine that is contrary to the glory of God’s grace, and the person of Christ… or what is repugnant to the experiences of God’s people.” The grammatical-historical Clarke names the root: “love to God and man is the root of the good tree, and from this principle all its fruit is found.” He turns the verse against antinomian teaching — to hold “that a state of salvation may be consistent with the greatest crimes… or that the righteous necessarily sin in all their best works, is really to make the good tree bring forth bad fruit, and to give the lie to the Author of eternal truth.”

The Wesleyan Wesley again steers the figure toward doctrine: “the goodness or badness here mentioned respects the doctrine, rather than the personal character,” since “a bad man preaching the good doctrine… is sometimes an instrument of converting sinners.” Yet he qualifies: he does not aver that all are true prophets who speak truth and convert, only that “none are such who do not.” The shared conviction is that nature determines fruit: a genuinely good root cannot, in the long run, yield what is contrary to its nature, nor a corrupt root what is good — so that the steady tendency of a man’s teaching and life is a reliable, God-given index of what he truly is, the very test by which the false prophet is unmasked. Clarke guards the truth from abuse: it does not mean the regenerate cannot sin, but that a genuinely good principle — “love to God and man” — cannot habitually issue in corrupt doctrine or a corrupt life, any more than a sound tree can yield a harvest of thorns. The impossibility is one of nature and tendency, not of every single act; yet it is sure enough to make the long fruit of a ministry a trustworthy verdict on its source.
19:
On Matthew 7:19 — “Every tree that bringeth not forth good fruit is hewn down, and cast into the fire” — the witnesses agree Christ here echoes the Baptist’s warning and turns it into a solemn threat against fruitless teachers and hearers.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes that the crowds “must, for the most part, have recognised the words as those which they had heard before from the lips of the Baptist” (Matthew 3:10), so that they served as “a link connecting the teaching of our Lord with that of the forerunner.” Clarke presses the present force of the judgment: “every tree that produceth not good fruit is to be now cut down; the act of excision is now taking place… the curse of the Lord is even now on the head and the heart of every false teacher, and impenitent hearer.” Barnes cross-references the same imagery in John 15:6.

The Wesleyan Wesley draws the application to ministry: “how dreadful then is the condition of that teacher who hath brought no sinners to God!” The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem, fittingly here, presses the warning on the hypocrite — “a man-pleaser… having the hypocrisy of Simon Magus” — bidding him learn from John that “now also the axe is laid unto the root of the trees… the Judge is inexorable; put away thine hypocrisy.” The verse, on every reading, leaves no neutral ground: the tree that fails to bear good fruit is not merely barren but doomed, the fruitless profession ending not in mild loss but in the fire — a sobering close to the figure of the two trees, and a bridge to the still graver warning of those who say “Lord, Lord” and are not known. Gill applies it directly to the unfaithful teacher — every “preacher and teacher that does not bring the Gospel of Christ with him… sooner or later” is cut down. Barnes ranks the saying with the Baptist’s and with the vine that, bearing no fruit, is gathered and burned (John 15:6), so that the warning against false prophets ends, like John’s preaching, at the axe and the fire.
20:
On Matthew 7:20 — “Wherefore by their fruits ye shall know them” — the witnesses take the repetition as a deliberate driving-home of the test already given in v.16.

The Reformed Gill reads it as “the conclusion of the whole, and a repetition… the more to fix the rule of judgment upon their minds,” engaging the hearers “to try men by their doctrines, and their doctrines by the standard of the Scriptures, and not believe every spirit” (1 John 4:1). He clarifies the sum: “ordinarily… as men are, so are the doctrines they preach, and by them they may be known” — Christ speaking here “not of men of bad lives” only but of false teachers whose teaching betrays them. The grammatical-historical Ellicott and Robertson note again the strengthened verb (epignōsesthe), “knowledge that is full, clear, decisive,” like that to which Paul looks forward (1 Corinthians 13:12).

Clarke marks why “this truth is often repeated”: “because our eternal interests depend so much upon it.” And he presses the sharp corollary of the tree-figure: “not to have good fruit is to have evil; there can be no innocent sterility in the invisible tree of the heart. He that brings forth no fruit, and he that brings forth bad fruit, are both only fit for the fire.” The threefold repetition of the test (vv. 16, 18, 20) thus fixes a rule simple enough for the weakest hearer yet weighty enough to decide eternal things: the steady fruit of doctrine and life, weighed by Scripture, discloses the true teacher from the false. Gill adds that with “some care and diligence such persons may be detected, and the malignant influence of their ministry be prevented” — the rule being given not to breed suspicion of all but to arm the simple against the few. Robertson and Ellicott alike note the verb is the strengthened “fully know,” a recognition as decisive as the harvest that proves the tree. Clarke adds that the truth is “often repeated because our eternal interests depend so much upon it” — the test of fruit being no incidental aside but the very hinge on which the discernment of true and false teachers turns.
21:
On Matthew 7:21 — “Not every one that saith unto me, Lord, Lord, shall enter into the kingdom of heaven; but he that doeth the will of my Father” — the witnesses agree profession without obedience is worthless, and several note how Christ here unveils himself as Judge.

The grammatical-historical and Reformed voices stress the priority of doing. Robertson marks the sharp contrast, “not… but,” between “the mere talker and the doer of God’s will.” Ellicott finds here strong confirmation of the ethical reading of “fruits”: “the nullity of the confession of a true faith… without doing the will of God” (compare John 7:17). Clarke explains the Hebraism “not every one” as “no person” who merely acknowledges Christ’s authority shall enter, “but he who doeth the will.” Wesley: “everything short of this is only saying, Lord, Lord.”

The fathers add depth. Jerome balances it against v.15: as those of good life but impious doctrine are refused, so are those of sound doctrine who “destroy it with evil works” (Titus 1:16). Augustine reconciles it with “no man can say that Jesus is Lord but by the Holy Ghost” (1 Corinthians 12:3): Paul means a saying that “expresses the will and understanding,” not mere lip-sound. Chrysostom hears Christ “censuring the Jews chiefly, who placed everything in dogmas,” and notes he says “the will of my Father” (not “my will”) in condescension to their weakness, though “there is no other will of the Son besides that of the Father” — and that “here in the end of his sermon he shows himself as the Judge.” Gregory draws the lesson that “charity and humility, not mighty works, are to be esteemed.” The Puritan Henry sums the sermon’s scope: “the religion he came to establish is in power, not in word only” (1 Corinthians 4:20), so that an outward profession, “however remarkable, will not bring us to heaven, unless” joined to obedience. Pseudo-Chrysostom defines the Father’s will from John 6:40 — to “see the Son and believe on him” — a believing that “has reference both to confession and conduct,” so that he who neither confesses Christ nor walks by his word “shall not enter.” (A stray Cyril of Jerusalem fragment on baptism is attached here.)
22:
On Matthew 7:22 — “Many will say to me in that day, Lord, Lord, have we not prophesied in thy name?… and in thy name done many wonderful works?” — the witnesses dwell on the day of judgment, the staggering claim Christ makes to be its Judge, and the truth that miracles are no proof of piety.

The grammatical-historical commentators feel the weight of the claim. Ellicott: “no part of the Sermon on the Mount is more marvellous in its claims” — at the very outset of his ministry Christ “claims, with the calmness of assured conviction, to be the Judge before whom the faithful and the hypocrites will alike give account” in “that day.” Barnes presses that “the power of working miracles had no necessary connexion with piety”: God may give “the power of raising the dead to a wicked man, as the skill of healing to a wicked physician.” Robertson notes the question with ou “expects the affirmative answer” — they really did prophesy and work wonders — “but Jesus will tear off the sheepskin and lay bare the ravening wolf,” for “success, as the world counts it, is not a criterion of one’s knowledge of Christ.” Clarke and Wesley list the claims — preaching, casting out demons, miracles — concluding “even the working of miracles is no proof that a man has saving faith.”

The Reformed Calvin reads it as Christ summoning hypocrites “to his judgment-seat”: so long as they hold a place in the Church “they both flatter themselves and deceive others,” but a day comes when he “will cleanse his barn, and separate the chaff… from the pure wheat.” Gill identifies the “many” as not common people only but “preachers of the word, who have filled the highest station in the church below.” The dreadful note, across the voices, is that the most dazzling gifts and the loudest confession may coexist with a heart Christ never knew. Hilary observes that the boasters “assure themselves of glory for their prophesying… for their casting out daemons, for their mighty works,” as though “they spoke somewhat of themselves,” not seeing that “the name of Christ casts out the daemons,” not they. Adam Clarke renders “in that very day” — the day of judgment when “the principles of all pretenders… shall be tried” — and lists their plea: preaching, casting out demons, doing “many miracles,” all urged as proof, all unavailing.
23:
On Matthew 7:23 — “Then will I profess unto them, I never knew you: depart from me, ye that work iniquity” — the witnesses agree the sentence is final and discriminating, turning on the meaning of “knew” and of the verb “profess.”

The grammatical-historical commentators define “I never knew you.” Barnes: “I never approved, loved, or regarded you as my friends” (2 Timothy 2:19; 1 Corinthians 8:3); Christ will not say he had once known them and then rejected them, “that they had been once Christians and then had fallen away… but that he had never known them — they had never been true Christians,” whatever their joys, raptures, or self-confidence. Clarke reads “I never approved of you… you held the truth in unrighteousness,” God blessing their preaching “for the sake of my own truth” while their persons he “could never esteem.” Robertson notes “profess” (homologēsō) is “the very word used of profession of Christ before men” (Matthew 10:32) — an open, public announcement.

Ellicott marks the verse as “a remarkable complement” to that promise of confessing his own before the Father: the condemnation falls not on those who “wandered from the truth” but on “workers of… lawlessness” (recalling Psalm 6:8). The Reformed Calvin: by “I will confess” Christ alludes to the hypocrites’ vain boasting; against their loud profession “I too will confess… that their profession is deceitful and false” — he “never reckoned them among his own people.” Wesley draws the searching question: “there never was a time that I approved of you… Lord, is it my case?” The close of the warning, on every reading, is that the decisive thing is not gifts, profession, or even apparent success, but whether Christ has truly known the man as his own. Clarke turns it on the reader and the preacher: “how many appear prophets in their pulpits” whose persons Christ “could never esteem, because destitute of the spirit of his Gospel, unholy in heart, and unrighteous in conduct.” Gill marks that the profession is made “publicly before men and angels, at the day of judgment” — and there found false. (The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note attached here strays to the two masters of Matthew 6:24.)
24:
On Matthew 7:24 — “whosoever heareth these sayings of mine, and doeth them, I will liken him unto a wise man, which built his house upon a rock” — the witnesses agree the parable’s point is hearing joined to doing, and several read the rock and the building allegorically.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the doing. Robertson: “that is the point… who digged and went deep, and laid a foundation upon the rock” (Luke 6:48). Barnes paints the Palestinian setting — a land of hills “subject to sudden and violent rains,” streams swelling furiously — so that only the house on rock stands. Ellicott marks the emphatic “every one whosoever,” and that the reference back to “these sayings” supports reading the chapters as “a continuous discourse.” Clarke cites the rabbinic parallels of Rabbi Eleazar — the man whose knowledge exceeds his works is “like a tree with many branches and few roots” that the wind uproots, while he whose works exceed his knowledge is “like a tree with few branches and many roots” that no wind can move.

The fathers allegorize. Pseudo-Chrysostom: the wise builder is likened to Christ, who built his house, “the Church, upon a rock, that is, upon the strength of the faith,” while the foolish is the Devil building on “the insecurity of unbelief.” Augustine reads the storms as evils — rain the “darkness of superstition,” winds the “rumours of men,” the flood “the lust of the flesh” — which none fears “who not only hears the command of the Lord, but who also does it,” for “no man confirms in himself what the Lord commands… but by doing it.” Hilary reads a rising assault of pleasures, then fiercer desires, then “the whole force of the driving tempests.”

The Reformed Calvin draws the comparison’s heart: two houses of “the same external appearance,” but the one without foundation “will immediately fall” under wind and flood, while the well-founded “will be sustained against every assault” — so a vain profession of the Gospel, however elevated, “wants a foundation.” Gill applies the “coming to Christ” of the Lukan parallel to the true hearer who believes and gives himself up to Christ.
25:
On Matthew 7:25 — “the rain descended, and the floods came, and the winds blew, and beat upon that house; and it fell not: for it was founded upon a rock” — the witnesses read the storm as the trials that test profession, and the rock as the security of the true believer.

The Reformed Gill expounds the several metaphors: rain, floods, stream, and winds “may design the temptations of Satan, the persecutions of the world, the corruptions of a man’s own heart, and the errors and false doctrines of men” — from all which the man built “on the rock Christ Jesus” is safe: the rain of temptation may descend “with great violence, but shall not beat him down”; the floods of persecution “shall not carry him away,” for “the gates of hell cannot prevail against him” (Psalm 92:13–15). The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the past perfect “was founded” (tethemeliōto) — “it had been built upon the rock and it stood.”

Clarke supplies the vivid eastern background: in Judea and near the tropics “the rain sometimes falls in great torrents, producing rivers, which sweep away the soil from the rocky hills; and the houses, built of brick only dried in the sun… literally melt away” before such rains. He distinguishes three trials to which believers are exposed — “temporal afflictions… like the torrents of rain,” the assaults of persecution, and the inner storms of temptation — the house on the rock standing through all. The settled sense across the witnesses is that the same storms beat on both houses; what saves is not exemption from trial but the hidden foundation, the doing of Christ’s words, which holds the soul fast when everything that can be shaken is shaken. Clarke distinguishes the “three general kinds of trials” to which the godly are exposed — temporal afflictions “like the torrents of rain,” the assaults of persecution like the rising floods, and the inward storms of temptation like the driving winds — the house on the rock standing through all three. Gill adds that “the gates of hell cannot prevail” against the man so founded; the same tempest that levels the unfounded house only proves the strength of the well-laid foundation.
26:
On Matthew 7:26 — “every one that heareth these sayings of mine, and doeth them not, shall be likened unto a foolish man, which built his house upon the sand” — the witnesses agree the fatal flaw is hearing without doing.

The Reformed Gill describes the foolish hearer: one who “only externally hears… but has no understanding” and does not believe or practise them, or who “depends upon his external hearing… and contents himself with a speculative knowledge, without the practice.” The grammatical-historical Robertson: “the foolish builder put his house on the sands that could not hold in the storm,” recalling the opening of the sermon (Matthew 5:19) about one “who does and teaches” — “hearing sermons is a dangerous business if one does not put them into practice.”

Clarke presses the warning home with force: “was there ever a stricter system of morality delivered by God to man than in this sermon?” He who hears it “and does not look to God to conform his soul and life to it,” yet hopes for heaven, “is like the fool who built his house on the sand”; for “talking about Christ, his righteousness, merits, and atonement, while the person is not conformed to his word and spirit, is no other than solemn self-deception.” The point, shared across the voices, is that the difference between the wise and the foolish builder is not in hearing — both hear the same sayings — nor in the storm, which falls on both, but solely in the doing: the sand is the very profession that rests on hearing alone, sure to give way when trial comes. Robertson recalls that the wise builder of Luke’s parallel “digged and went deep,” while the foolish merely set his house “on the sands that could not hold” — the contrast lying wholly in the unseen foundation, not in the visible structure. Clarke presses that to hear “so strict a system of morality” and not look to God “to conform the soul and life to it,” while still hoping for heaven, is the very folly of the sand-builder — a hope that the first real storm will bury under its own ruins.
27:
On Matthew 7:27 — “the rain descended, and the floods came… and it fell: and great was the fall of it” — the witnesses dwell on the certainty and greatness of the ruin that overtakes the house without foundation.

The Reformed Gill catalogues the storms once more: such a builder “cannot stand against the violent rain of Satan’s temptations, the floods of the world’s persecutions, the stream and rapid torrent of their own heart’s lusts… and much less the storms of divine wrath and vengeance.” Their condition is “most dangerous”: “they must fall, and great will be their fall; their destruction is inevitable, their ruin irrecoverable.” Barnes cross-references the testing fire of 1 Corinthians 3:13 and the dreadful fall of Hebrews 10:26–27.

The grammatical-historical Clarke gives a striking illustration: the fishermen of Bengal, who in the dry season “build their huts on the beds of sand from which the rivers had retired”; but when the rains set in suddenly and “the waters pour down in torrents from the mountains, in one night multitudes of these buildings are swept away, and the place where they stood is on the next morning indiscoverable.” So, the witnesses agree, with the soul founded on hearing alone: its collapse is not gradual but sudden and total, and the “great” fall measures the height of a profession that had risen impressively yet rested on nothing. The parable thus seals the whole sermon with a solemn alternative — the same words heard, the same storms endured, but two destinies parted forever by whether the hearer also did. Gill enumerates once more the storms that overwhelm such a builder — Satan’s temptations, the world’s persecutions, the heart’s lusts, false doctrine, and at last “the storms of divine wrath and vengeance” — against which there is no standing. Barnes hears in “great was the fall of it” the dreadful ruin of those who, having known the way, draw back (Hebrews 10:26–27); the height of the collapse measuring the emptiness of the profession it overthrew. Clarke’s image of the Bengal fishermen’s sand-huts, swept away in a single night so that “the place where they stood is on the next morning indiscoverable,” fixes the suddenness of the ruin: the storm that comes upon the unfounded soul leaves not even a trace of the structure that hearing-without-doing had raised.
28:
On Matthew 7:28 — “when Jesus had ended these sayings, the people were astonished at his doctrine” — the witnesses note the close of the discourse, the nature of the crowd’s wonder, and the unity of the sermon.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the manner of the astonishment. Ellicott renders it “astonished at his teaching,” with “greater prominence given… to its manner than to its substance,” and takes “when Jesus had ended these sayings” as pointing again to “one continuous discourse.” Robertson glosses the imperfect (exeplēssonto, “were struck out of themselves”) as “a buzz of astonishment” — they “listened spell-bound to the end and were left amazed.” Clarke notes the “multitudes” heard the law “defined in such a manner as they had never thought of before… urged home on their consciences with such clearness and authority.”

The fathers probe the quality of the wonder. Pseudo-Chrysostom distinguishes praise (when the mind is “satisfied reasonably”) from wonder (when it is “overcome”), and observes that their admiration “pertained rather to Christ’s glory than to their faith, for had they believed… they would not have wondered” — and it was “the multitude,” the simple folk, who wondered, not the chief among the people. Chrysostom marvels that, though the sayings were lofty and hard, “so great was the power of the Teacher” that the hearers were “caught” and followed him down the mountain. Augustine raises the harmony with Luke’s plain-sermon, supposing one discourse recorded by both “in different words, but with the same truth of substance.” The Reformed Calvin: “a strange, indescribable, and unwonted majesty drew to him the minds of men,” the “these sayings” including not the mount-discourse only but all the doctrine he had made known. Gill rehearses the sermon’s whole sweep — true happiness, the duty of ministers, the law’s true sense, alms and prayer and fasting, rash judging, the two ways, false prophets, and right hearing — as the “sayings” now ended. Rabanus reads the wonder two ways: it “either signifies the unbelieving… astonished because they did not believe” or all of them, “in that they reverenced… so great wisdom.”
29:
On Matthew 7:29 — “for he taught them as one having authority, and not as the scribes” — the witnesses agree this names the ground of the astonishment: a teaching authority wholly unlike the scribes’ derivative manner.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw the contrast sharply. Lightfoot: the scribes “borrowed credit to their doctrine from traditions, and the fathers of them”; no scribe’s sermon had “authority or value” without “the Rabbins have a tradition” or “the wise men say.” Robertson, citing the Mishna and Gemara as “the driest, dullest collection” of comments, notes the scribes “were afraid to express an idea without bolstering it up by some predecessor,” whereas “Jesus spoke with the authority of truth… and the power of God’s Spirit.” Ellicott points to the marks of that authority already seen — the “I say unto you” set against what was “said to them of old time,” and the assumption that the speaker is “the Head of the divine kingdom and the Judge of quick and dead” — and to “the entire absence of any reference by name to” other interpreters. Clarke notes the variant adding “and the Pharisees,” and that the people “felt a commanding power… perspicuous… persuasive… irresistible.”

The fathers ground the authority in his person. Jerome: “as the God and Lord of Moses himself,” he “of his own free will” added to or changed the Law (“but I say unto you”), while the scribes “only taught what was written in Moses and the Prophets.” Chrysostom: not, like the prophets, “thus saith the Lord,” but as one whose own word carried the power. The Wesleyan Wesley: he taught “with a dignity and majesty peculiar to himself as the great Lawgiver, and with the demonstration and power of the Spirit… not as the scribes, who only expounded the law of another, and that in a lifeless, ineffectual manner.” The Sermon thus ends, the witnesses agree, with the hearers’ testimony to the very thing it everywhere displayed: the unborrowed, divine authority of the Teacher. Gill notes the contrast lay not only in the weight of his matter — so unlike the scribes’ trivial handling of “the rituals of the law… the traditions of the elders” — but in his “great affection, ardour… liberty and utterance… and majesty.” Robertson contrasts the scribes’ fear “to express an idea without bolstering it up by some predecessor” with Christ, who “spoke with the authority of truth… the freshness of the morning light, and the power of God’s Spirit.” So the multitudes, untaught in the schools, felt at once a commanding power the learned scribes had never given them.
